coredata.dc:title: A novel graphene quantum dots/choline chloride/gold nanoparticles-modified carbon fiber microelectrode for sensitive and selective determination of dopamine in the presence of a high concentration of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lili
Wang, Tingting
Bao, Changhao
Shi, Min
Huang, Xianju
Cheng, Han
coredata.dc:description: We prepared a three-layer composite of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), choline (Ch) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and applied it to modify a carbon fiber microelectrode (CFME) by potentiostatic deposition to obtain a GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME for high-sensitivity determination of dopamine (DA) without inference from ascorbic acid (AA). The surface morphology of electrodes was investigated by scanning electron microscopy. The electrochemical behavior of DA and AA at different modified electrodes was investigated by cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry in Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9.0). Owing to a synergistic effect, the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME exhibited an obvious electrocatalytic effect on positively charged DA and a significant shielding effect on negatively charged AA. The influence of experimental variables (duration of modification, pH of the working solution, scan rate) was studied. Under optimum conditions, in the presence of AA (1.0 × 10−3 mol/L), the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME described a fine linear enhancement for DA oxidation in the concentration range 0.01–10.0 μM with an estimated lower limit of detection of 1.616 nM and limit of quantification of 5.389 nM. The modified electrochemical sensor was applied for DA determination in the presence of high concentration of AA in mouse serum without separation steps and with good recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Choline chloride
Graphene quantum dots
Carbon fiber microelectrode
Dopamine
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and electrochemical properties of the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME
Optimization of the electrodeposition time of the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME
Influence of the scan rate and pH
Selective detection of different charged substances by the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME
Selective detection of DA by the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME in the presence of a high concentration of AA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) CFME (B) AuNPs/CFME (C) Ch/AuNPs/CFME (D) GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME (E). Nyquist plots for the bare CFME (black curve), AuNPs/CFME (blue curve), Ch/AuNPs/CFME (green curve) and GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME (red curve) in 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/ K4[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1 M KCl. The inset is the simple circuit model used to fit Nyquist plots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
DPV curves of the CFME, AuNPs/CFME, Ch/AuNPs/CFME and GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME in 1.0 × 10−5 mol/L DA (0.1 mol/L Tris-HCl pH = 7.0).
Deposition time and oxidation peak current in DA (1 × 10−5 mol/L) by the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME. Error bars show the standard deviation (n = 3).

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of DA (1 × 10−5 mol/L) with the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME at different scan rates (10, 20, 50, 100, 250 mV/s). (B) Plot of the anodic and cathodic peak currents of DA versus the scan rate. (C) Plot of the anodic and cathodic peak potentials of DA versus the logarithm of the scan rate. Measurements were made in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0). Cyclic voltammetry was carried out within the potential range from − 0.2 V to 0.6 V.
Cyclic voltammograms of DA (1 × 10−5 mol/L) in Tris-HCl buffer solution at different pH (A) and the relationship between the oxidation peak current and pH (B).

Cyclic voltammograms at the carbon fiber microelectrode (CFME) in two redox mediators before and after modification: (A) DA (1.0 × 10−5 mol/L). (B) AA (1.0 × 10−3 mol/L). The blue curves show the electrochemical responses of Tris-HCl buffer by the CFME and GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME. The black curves show the electrochemical responses at the CFME. The red curves show the electrochemical responses at the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME. Measurements were made at a scan rate of 100 mV/s in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) DPV curves showing the oxidation peak current in DA (5.0 μmol/L) containing 1000-fold K+, Na+, SO4 2− or Cl− with the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME (n = 3). (B)the current response of the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME (n = 5) toward DA (5.0 μmol/L) for 7 consecutive days.

(A) DPV at the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME in a mixed solution of DA and AA(1.0 × 10−3 mol/L). The DA concentration is 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 μmol/L at a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, j, k, l, m, n, o, p. (B) Plot of oxidation current versus DA concentration.
Mechanism of electrochemical redox of DA on the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME.
Comparison of the results of different electrodes for the determination of dopamine in the presence of high concentrations of ascorbic acid.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Polymerized DL-phenylalanine/Graphite electrode (Poly(DLPA)/GE). Poly (adenine)/Carbon nanotube paste electrode (Poly (adenine)/CNTPE). Monoclinic-Tungsten trioxide/Glassy carbon electrode (γ-WO3/GCE). NiFe2O4-Activated carbon/Glassy carbon electrode (NiFe2O4-AC/AC). Graphene oxide/TmPO4/Glassy carbon electrode (GO/TmPO4/GCE). Au-Graphene quantum dots-Nafion/Glassy carbon electrode (Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE). Gold nanoparticles/Reduced graphene oxide/Glassy carbon electrode (AuNP/rGO/GCE). L-Cysteine/Glassy carbon electrode (L-Cysteine/GCE). Copper hexacyanocobaltate/Glassy carbon electrode (CuHCC/GCE). Poly-beta-cyclodextrin (Functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes)/polyaniline/Glassy carbon electrode (poly-β-CD(f-MWCNTs)/PANI/GCE). Graphene/Glassy carbon electrode (GR/GCE). Linear sweep stripping voltammetry (LSSV).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of dopamine in the presence of a high concentration of ascorbic acid in a mouse serum sample and dopamine-hydrochloride injection.

head.ce:title: A novel graphene quantum dots/choline chloride/gold nanoparticles-modified carbon fiber microelectrode for sensitive and selective determination of dopamine in the presence of a high concentration of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, South Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, South Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: South-Central University for Nationalities, 182 Minyuan Road, Hongshan District, Wuhan, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We prepared a three-layer composite of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), choline (Ch) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and applied it to modify a carbon fiber microelectrode (CFME) by potentiostatic deposition to obtain a GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME for high-sensitivity determination of dopamine (DA) without inference from ascorbic acid (AA). The surface morphology of electrodes was investigated by scanning electron microscopy. The electrochemical behavior of DA and AA at different modified electrodes was investigated by cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry in Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9.0). Owing to a synergistic effect, the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME exhibited an obvious electrocatalytic effect on positively charged DA and a significant shielding effect on negatively charged AA. The influence of experimental variables (duration of modification, pH of the working solution, scan rate) was studied. Under optimum conditions, in the presence of AA (1.0 × 10−3 mol/L), the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME described a fine linear enhancement for DA oxidation in the concentration range 0.01–10.0 μM with an estimated lower limit of detection of 1.616 nM and limit of quantification of 5.389 nM. The modified electrochemical sensor was applied for DA determination in the presence of high concentration of AA in mouse serum without separation steps and with good recovery.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is an important type of catecholamine, distributed widely in the central nervous system. It is used to transmit messages, maintain hormonal balance, control emotion and plays an important part in the function of the central nervous, cardiovascular, renal, and hormonal systems [1,2]. Determination of DA is crucial for investigating its physiological functions and diagnosing nervous-system diseases resulting from the abnormal metabolism of DA. Insufficient or excess amounts of DA can lead to disorders in mammals, such as epilepsy, senile dementia, parkinsonism, and schizophrenia [3].

However, a major problem in DA determination is interference from ascorbic acid (AA), which largely coexists with DA in brain issue. At conventional solid electrodes, AA is oxidized at potentials close to that of DA, which results in over-lapped voltametric responses, which makes their discrimination highly difficult [4,5]. Moreover, very often, solid electrodes suffer from a fouling effect due to the accumulation of oxidized products upon their surface, which results in poor selectivity and sensitivity. Therefore, development of new electrochemical sensors for DA has garnered much interest in recent decades [6].

Choline (Ch) is a strong organic base. It is a component of lecithin, and also exists in the sphingomyelin of nerve cells. Ch is the product of methyl synthesis, and a precursor of acetylcholine [7]. Recently, there has been considerable interest in developing Ch-modified electrodes for electroanalysis [8–10]. Ch contains –OH and –N+(CH3)3 groups. The –OH group of Ch enables it to be immobilized or assembled onto the surface of carbon electrodes through covalent linkage [11]. Furthermore, the –N+(CH3)3 polar head group of Ch can provide a positively charged and uniform surface of suitable density [12]. This feature, can facilitate deposition of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through the electrostatic interaction between–N+(CH3)3 and GQDs with a negative surface charge, and also decrease the resistance of the electron transfer between GQDs and electrode surface.

Metal nanoparticles (NPs), carbon-based nanomaterials, and biocompatible polymers have been used extensively to modify working electrodes [13–15], due to their high electrocatalytic activity and large active surface area. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are excellent electrocatalytic materials for electrochemical determination towards various materials due to superior conductivity, high specific surface area, and excellent catalytic properties [16]. GQDs, are zero-dimensional nano materials with a plane size < 100 nm and thickness < 10 layers [17]. They have aroused increasing attention in biomedical fields because of their small size, chemical inertness, stable photoluminescence, low toxicity and biocompatibility [18]. Furthermore, GQDs have unique properties, including high surface area, conductivity, and good dispersibility, which enables application of GQDs in energy-related applications [19,20]. In particular, GQDs have negatively charged carboxyl groups, which offer good stability and could interact electrostatically with the amine groups in DA. Moreover, π-π stacking between GQDs and DA makes electron transfer and electron communication stronger [21].

Here, a carbon fiber microelectrode (CFME) was modified by a three-layer composite (AuNPs-Ch-GQDs) and used for examination of voltametric behavior and selective determination of trace amounts of DA. By optimizing the electrodeposition time, a modified electrode with good stability and electrocatalytic performance was obtained. The GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME had a significant electrocatalytic effect on DA and had a simultaneous shielding effect on AA. Also, compared with most reported detectors, our CFME had a wider detection range for DA in the presence of AA, and the limit of detection (LoD) was lower.

Carbon fibers (diameter = 5 μm) were purchased from Jilin Shenzhou Carbon Fiber (Jilin, China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·H2O), sodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O) and potassium carbonate (K2CO3) were obtained from the National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Tannic acid (C76H52O64) and Ch (C5H15NO2) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology (Shanghai, China). Quantum dots were obtained from Nanjing Xianfeng Nano Material Technology (Nanjing, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade. A toner-conductive adhesive was prepared by our research team. Measurements were made at pH 7.0 and pH 9.0 Tris-HCl was the buffer and used at room temperature. Water was double-distilled.

Characterization of samples was carried out using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) based on, a SU8010 system, (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), an inverted microscope (XD-RFL; Ningbo Haoyu Instruments, Zhejiang, China), and a pH meter (PHS-3C; Shanghai Shenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China). The electrochemical signal was measured on a CHI 660D Chemstation (Shanghai Shenhua Instruments). The Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode (Shanghai Ruosull Technology, Shanghai, China).

Under stirring, 20 mL of distilled water, 0.56 mL of 1% trisodium citrate solution, 0.3 mL of 1% tannic acid, potassium carbonate solution, and 0.5 mL of 1% HAuCl4 solution were mixed sequentially at room temperature and stirred until the solution was burgundy and did not change color. AuNPs were obtained and preserved at 4 ℃ until use.

Preparation of the CFME modified with AuNPs, Ch and GQDs (GQDs/Ch/AuNPs) to be used in electrochemical determination was carried out in one-step potentiostatic electrodeposition. Fist, the Ag/AgCl reference electrode and prepared CFME were placed in AuNPs solution and electrodeposited at a constant potential of + 1.5 V for 15 min. The AuNPs/CFME obtained, was washed with ultrapure water. Then, the AuNPs/CFME was immersed in Ch solution (2 mmol/L) to modify it for 15 min to obtain Ch/AuNPs/CFME. After cleaning, the Ch/AuNPs/CFME was placed in aqueous dispersions of GQDs and electrodeposited for 10 min. At the end of these processes, the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME was obtained and washed with ultrapure water.
For the sake of comparison, a AuNPs/CFME and Ch/AuNPs/CFME were prepared under identical conditions.

For Electrochemical determination of DA, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were undertaken in Tris-HCl (0.1 mol/L) at pH 9.0. The GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME was used as a working electrode and the Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode. DPV was employed at a potential sweep range of −0.2 V to 0.6 V, an amplitude of 0.05 V, a pulse width of 0.05 s, pulse duration of 0.2 s, and resting time of 20 s. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were carried out across a potential range from −0.2 V to 0.6 V using a scan rate of 100 mV/s. Each measurement was undertaken using a new working electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and electrochemical properties of the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME
ce:section.ce:para: The surface of the bare electrode was smooth, and NPs were absorbed on the electrode surface after modification with AuNPs (Fig. 1 B). There was no obvious change on the electrode surface after modification with Ch (Fig. 1C). GQDs were well distributed, homogeneously distributed, and covered the whole GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME (Fig. 1D).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective method for probing the features of a surface-modified electrode using the redox probe Fe(CN)6 4−/3−, in KCl solution [22]. EIS was employed to investigate the impedance changes on the electrode surface during modification. Fig. 1E shows the Nyquist plots of K3Fe(CN6)/K4Fe(CN)6 on the bare CFME (black), AuNPs/CFME (blue), Ch/AuNPs/CFME (green) and GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME (red). The semicircle diameter observed in the higher-frequency zone of the Nyquist plots revealed the electron transfer resistance at the electrode surface. Observation of a semicircle with a smaller diameter on the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME surface demonstrated a lower electron transfer resistance for the modified electrode.

The electrochemical performance of DA on the as-prepared electrodes were investigated first using DPV. Fig. 2 shows DPV of the CFME, AuNPs/CFME, Ch/AuNPs/CFME and GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME in Tris-HCl (0.1 mol/L, pH = 7.0) containing of DA (10 μmol/L). There was no obvious peak on the bare CFME. A well-defined oxidation peak was observed at 200 mV (DA) at the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME, which was much more prominent than that using the AuNPs/CFME or Ch/AuNPs/CFME. Such a notable peak on the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME could have been due to Ch possessing a hydroxy group (which could bind covalently to the edge plane sites of the carbon surface through an oxygen atom [11]), and good performance of AuNPs and GQDs (which could provide active sites to promote electron transfer and enhance adsorption strength toward biomolecules).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the electrodeposition time of the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME
ce:section.ce:para: AuNPs, Ch, and GQDs were electropolymeriz on the CFME using a potentiostatic time–current curve method at an initial potential of 1.5 V. An essential factor for improving the performance of an electrochemical sensor is the surface area of the electrode. Fig. 3 shows the effect of electrodeposition time on the oxidation peak current of DA (n = 3). First, the CFME was electrodeposited with AuNPs. As the electrodeposition time increased from 5 min to 15 min, the peak current continued to increase and, finally, reached a maximum value at the end of this stage. Subsequently, the oxidation peak current decreased with increasing electrodeposition time. This phenomenon occurred because the AuNPs continued to aggregate on the electrode surface as the electrodeposition time increased. When the electrodeposition time exceeded 15 min, large aggregated particles fell off the electrode surface, resulting in a decreased specific surface area of the modified electrode. In addition, the deposition times for coating of Ch and GQDs on the AuNPs/CFME electrode were optimized. A maximum DA redox current could be obtained by the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME at a deposition time of 15 min (AuNPs), 15 min (Ch), and 10 min (GQDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the scan rate and pH
ce:section.ce:para:
To better understand the electrochemical mechanism of DA on the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME, some electrochemical parameters of DA on the modified electrode were investigated further. The effect of the scan rate on the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME was investigated with CV in DA (1 × 10−5 mol/L) (Fig. 4 ) at different scan rates (10, 20, 50, 100, 250 mV/s). With an increase in the scan rate, the anodic peak current (Ipa) and cathodic peak current (Ipc) of DA increased gradually. The Ipa and Ipc of DA was proportional to the scan rate (Fig. 4B). The regression equation between the Ipa or Ipc and the scan rate could be expressed, respectively, as:
Ipa (nA) = −233.53 × 10−3 − 33.6 × 10−3V (mV/s), R2 = 0.994
Ipc (nA) = 670.5 × 10−3 + 14.6 × 10−3V (mV/s), R2 = 0.997

These results indicated that the electrochemical redox behavior of DA on the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME surface was controlled by adsorption. In addition, it is clear from Fig. 4A that, with an increase in the scan rate, the anodic peak potential (Epa) of DA shifted positively, whereas the cathodic peak potential (Epc) shifted negatively. The relationship between the Epa or Epc and the base-10 logarithm of scan rate (lgv) could comply (Fig. 4C) with a regression equation of:
Epa (mV) = 155.28 + 23.056lgv (mV/s), R2 = 0.990
Epc (mV) = 193.07–13.018lgv (mV/s), R2 = 0.992

According to the Laviron equation, the electron transfer coefficient (α) and electron transfer number (n) can be calculated as follows [23]:
E
pa
=
E
θ ′
+
2.3 R T
( 1 - ∂ ) n F
lg v
E
pc
=
E
θ ′
-
2.3 R T
∂ n F
lg v
where Eθ’, R, F, and T represent the formal redox potential, ideal gas constant, Faraday constant, and thermodynamic temperature, respectively. On the basis of the above-mentioned equations, from the slope of the plot of Epa or Epc versus lgv, the value of a and n values was estimated to be 0.64 and 1.92, respectively. This result suggested that two electrons were involved in the electrochemical oxidation of DA, data which are consistent with results from other reports [24].

Using CV, the effect of solution pH on the redox peak potential of DA was investigated at the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME (Fig. 5 ). The Epa and Epc of DA shifted towards a negative potential with an increase in pH, indicating that protons were involved in the direct oxidation reaction of DA (Fig. 5A). The latter is a graph of the Epa versus pH, and the linear regression equation was found to be Epa (mV) = 733.16–53.618pH, with R2 = 0.9901. This result indicates that the Epa of DA was pH-dependent, with a slope of − 53.618 mV/pH unit in a range of 4.0 to 10.0, which is close to the Nernstian value of − 59/n mV. Therefore, the ratio of electrons: protons was 2:2 and as-oxidized DA-o-quinone products were obtained [20,25], Scheme 1 described the possible reaction mechanisms. The maximum peak oxidation current was achieved at pH 9.0 (Fig. 5). Thus, for the subsequent voltametric analysis, 0.1 mol/L Tris-HCl at pH 9.0 was selected as the supporting electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of different charged substances by the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical characterization of the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME was studied with CV in two redox mediators. The cyclic voltammograms of two different electrodes were recorded in a solution of DA (1.0 × 10−5 mol/L) and in a solution of AA (1.0 × 10−3 mol/L), respectively. The GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME (red curve) showed a significantly increased redox peak current compared with the CFME (black curve) in DA (1.0 × 10−5 mol/L) (Fig. 6 A). Meanwhile, the oxidation peak and reduction peak shifted positively, and electrodes showed a smaller potential difference between oxidation and reduction peaks (ΔEp) in the DA solution. Hence the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME exhibited significant electrocatalytic activity towards the redox reaction of DA. There was a well-defined oxidation peak in AA (1.0 × 10−3 mol/L) at the bare CFME, whereas a redox peak was observed with the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME (Fig. 6B). These data indicated that the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME could shield the electrochemical response of AA and achieve sensitive and selective detection of DA.

To apply the developed sensor for DA determination in blood plasma samples, the influence of various possible interfering species present in plasma was investigated. The effects of K+, Na+, SO4 2− or Cl− on the analytical signal of a standard solution of DA (5.0 µM) were evaluated. Three replicate measurements were carried out for each interference concentration. For DA, the oxidation peak current was virtually unchanged, and interference was not detected in the presence of 1000-fold K+, Na+, SO4 2− and Cl− (Fig. 7 A).

The stability of the modified electrodes (n = 5) was evaluated by observing the response of the modified electrodes in DA (5.0 μmol/L) at pH 9.0 in Tris-HCl. Experiments were repeated for 7 consecutive days (Fig. 7B). The final peak current of different batches of the modified electrode was < 4.6%. Hence, the modified electrode was very stable and reliable. The half-life of the electrode was > 1 year.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of DA by the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME in the presence of a high concentration of AA
ce:section.ce:para: DPV offers a higher current response and better resolution than CV. DPV was utilized to examine the sensitivity, LoD and limit of quantification (LoQ) of the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME for DA assessment in the presence of AA. Fig. 8 A shows the differential pulse voltammograms at various DA concentrations in the presence of AA (1 × 10−3 mol/L). Fig. 8B provides the calibration curve for DA determination. The oxidation peak current of DA was proportional to its concentration, ranging from 0.01 μmol/L to 10 μmol/L. A linear regression equation could be obtained as Ip (nA) = 4631 × 10−4C (μmol/L) + 858 × 10−4, with R2 = 0.9979. The LoD and LoQ of the modified electrode was obtained using the equations of LoD = 3σ/b and LoQ = 10σ/b, respectively, where, σ signifies the standard deviation of the blank and b denotes the slope of the calibration curve. The LoD and LoQ was 1.616 nM and 5.389 nM, respectively, indicating that the modified electrode catalyzed DA oxidation, and that, the presence of AA did not interfere with the DA signal. Furthermore, the method had high sensitivity, low LoD and a wide linear range, which would be suitable for analyses of DA in complex biological samples.

Voltametric determination of DA in the presence of a high concentration of AA obtained with the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME was extremely satisfactory compared with that of other DA sensors reported in the literature (Table 1 ). The modified electrode had superior properties: simplicity of preparation, low cost, good sensitivity, and high selectivity. Furthermore, Table 1 indicates that the linear range was comparable with that of other reports, and that the LoD of the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME in this work was lower than that of modified electrodes reported in the literature.

The applicability and feasibility of the constructed GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME for DA detection were evaluated by a standard addition method. Mouse serum (2 mL) was diluted to 20 mL with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9.0). An injection of DA hydrochloride (standard concentration of DA = 52.7 mmol/L) was obtained from Shanghai Harvest Pharmaceuticals (Shanghai, China). The injection of DA hydrochloride was diluted to the desired volume with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9.0) for the analytical test. Then, AA solution (0.2 mmol) was added to the sample solution. For the mouse-serum sample, the DA recovery was 96.0%–101.1%. For the DA-hydrochloride injection, DA recovery was 96.8%–101.1% (Table 2 ). In addition, the relative standard of five parallel determinations using electrodes from different batches was < 5%. Hence the GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME has good reproducibility and was free from interference by AA, which can be applied for determination in real samples.

Efficient electrochemical composites were fabricated based on modification of a CFME with AuNPs, Ch and GQDs (GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME) for DA determination. The GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME showed a high selectivity for DA in the presence of AA due to the electrostatic repulsion between AA and GQDs/Ch/AuNPs. A significant transfer of two electrons and two protons in the mechanism of DA on GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME with a diffusion-controlled process was documented. The proposed method for DA had a low LoD, acceptable fabrication reproducibility and stability, and excellent reusability. The proposed electrode was applied to the determination of DA in mouse serum samples. This prepared GQDs/Ch/AuNPs/CFME could be employed in DA analyses in biological microenvironments.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21205144), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (CZZ16004, CZY18036, CZH20002).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Micellar effects on the electrochemistry of dopamine and its selective detection in the presence of ascorbic acid
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. L. Wen, Y. H. Jia, Z. L. Liu, Micellar effects on the electrochemistry of dopamine and its selective detection in the presence of ascorbic acid, Talanta 50 (1999) 1027–1033.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of dopamine and serotonin on a glassy carbon electrode coated with a film of carbon nanotubes
Anal. Biochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Wu, J. Fei, S. Hu, Simultaneous determination of dopamine and serotonin on a glassy carbon electrode coated with a film of carbon nanotubes, Anal. Biochem. 318 (2003) 100–106.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of layer-by-layer modified multilayer films containing choline and gold nanoparticles and its sensing application for electrochemical determination of dopamine and uric acid
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wang, Y. Li, X. Huang, L. Wang, Fabrication of layer-by-layer modified multilayer films containing choline and gold nanoparticles and its sensing application for electrochemical determination of dopamine and uric acid, Talanta 73 (2007) 431–437.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, uric acid and neurotransmitters with a carbon ceramic electrode prepared by sol–gel technique
Talanta
